🎯 Scope of Practice

🎯
Pre-Registration Nurse — Scope of Practice
▾

Your Educator and respective Buddy Nurses provide direction whilst on placement. IV pumps and medications cannot be handled without direct supervision, even for basic checks.

Without direct supervision
RAT's / PCR tests Assist mobilisation BGL via glucometer Vital signs / obs ABCDE assessment
With supervision
Subcut / IM injections & venepuncture Antibiotic / IV medication prep Simple / complex wound dressings
NMBA Decision-Making FrameworkRead the full national framework defining scope of practice for nurses and midwives.
🔗
NMBA Frameworks & Decision-Making Framework
nursingmidwiferyboard.gov.au
›

📋 Community Nursing — Practice Standards

📋
NSQHS Standards (8 Standards)
▾

Community Nursing Practice Standards are a subset of the National Safety and Quality Health Service Standards.

Clinical Governance Partnering with Consumers Preventing & Controlling HAI Medication Safety Comprehensive Care Communicating for Safety Blood Management Recognising & Responding to Acute Deterioration
🔗
ACSQHC — NSQHS Standards
safetyandquality.gov.au
›

🧼 Handwashing

Improving hand hygiene among healthcare workers is key to minimising the spread of antibiotic-resistant organisms within hospitals and patient homes.

🗣️ ISBAR

ISBAR — for safe, comprehensive and unambiguous communication

Identify · Situation · Background · Assessment · Recommendation

⚠️ A-PINCH Medications

A-PINCH identifies a group of high-risk, time-critical medications most likely to cause catastrophic patient harm if used in error.

A — Anticoagulants P — Potassium & other electrolytes I — Insulin N — Narcotics & other sedatives C — Chemotherapeutic agents H — Heparin & other anticoagulants

✅ 9 Rights of Medication Administration

The 9 RIGHTS of medication administration

🧮 Drug Calculations — Volume Required

Complete the Volume Required column for each scenario. Use Formula 1 (SR ÷ SS × Volume of Stock) — see the Drug Calculations page for worked examples and calculators.

Strength Required Stock Volume Required
a1000 units1000 units / 2mL
b1.25 micrograms2.5 micrograms / 1mL
c20mg100mg / 5mL
d4g1g / 4mL
e30mg in 20mL volume (NaCl 0.9%)10mg / mL
—correct out of 5

⚡ Anaphylaxis — Diagrams

Management of Anaphylaxis Algorithm — Community Setting

SVHM community setting algorithm — IM adrenaline 0.5mg/0.5mL, repeat every 5 minutes as needed

🩹 Simple / Dry Dressings

🩹
Simple Island Dressings
▾
Material
Absorbent pad with adhesive backing
Advantages
Closed wounds / post-opAbsorbs small exudate
Disadvantages
Not for open/high-exudate wounds
Wound Types
Closed surgical woundsPrimary intention
Examples
Alldress®Primapore®
🛡️
Non-Adherent Wound Contact Layer
▾
Material
Moist dressings with paraffin or compounds
Advantages
Protects wound bedNon-adherent
Disadvantages
Limited exudate absorptionNeeds secondary dressing
Wound Types
Superficial woundsBurns
Examples
Mepitel®Atraumann®
🫧
Films & Membranes
▾
Material
Transparent plastic films/membranes
Advantages
Vapour permeableWound visibility
Disadvantages
Limited exudate absorption
Wound Types
EpithelialisingLow-exudate superficial
Examples
Hydrofilm®Tegaderm®

💧 Moist Dressings

💎
Hydrocolloid Dressings
▾
Material
Hydrocolloid film/sheet — locks moisture
Advantages
Maintains hydration
Disadvantages
Risk of skin maceration
Wound Types
Low–medium exudate
Examples
Tegaderm Hydrocolloid®
💧
Hydrogel Dressings
▾
Material
Water-based gel (60–70%)
Advantages
Breaks down necrotic tissue
Disadvantages
Maceration if overusedNeeds secondary dressing
Wound Types
Desiccated / necrotic tissue
Examples
Kerralite Cool®Intrasite Gel®

🧽 Absorbent Dressings

🌊
Hydrofiber Dressings
▾
Material
Hydrofiber — converts to soft gel
Advantages
Moderate–high exudateAutolytic debridement
Disadvantages
Can stick to wound edges
Wound Types
Medium–high exudate
Examples
Aquacel®
🌿
Alginate Dressings
▾
Material
Seaweed-derived alginate — forms gel
Advantages
HaemostaticAbsorbs & retains fluid
Disadvantages
Can dry out woundOdour
Wound Types
Medium–high exudate
Examples
Algisite M®ActivHeal® Alginate
🫁
Foam Dressings
▾
Material
Gel-foam matrix removes exudate from bed
Advantages
Manages excess exudateAdhesive or non-adhesive
Disadvantages
Odour when wetCan strip fragile skin
Wound Types
Medium–high exudate
Examples
ActivHeal® FoamAllevyn Gentle®

🔬 Other Specialised

🦠
Antimicrobial Dressings
▾
Material
Honey, iodine or silver
Advantages
Kills surface bacteriaReduces odour
Disadvantages
Allergy riskResistance risk
Wound Types
Local infection / colonisation
Examples
Inadine®Activon Tulle®
💨
Odour Absorbent Dressings
▾
Material
Odour-sealing materials
Advantages
Reduces wound odour
Disadvantages
Stops functioning when wet
Wound Types
Severely malodorous wounds
Examples
CarboFLEX®
Selection tipLow exudate → film/hydrocolloid. Medium–high → foam/alginate/hydrofiber. Necrotic → hydrogel. Infected → antimicrobial. Malodorous → odour absorbent.
Formula 1 — Tablets / Oral & Injectable Doses
Volume Required = Strength Required (SR)Stock Strength (SS) × Volume of Stock

💡 Memory tip: SUNRISE (SR) over SUNSET (SS) — required strength always on top.

Formula 2 — IV Drip Rate (Drops/Minute)
Drip Rate (DPM) = Volume (mL) × Drops/mLTime (h) × 60

Always convert litres to mL (×1000) before calculating.

Formula 3 — IV Infusion: Volume, Rate & Time
Volume = Rate × Time  |  Rate = VolumeTime  |  Time = VolumeRate

Worked Examples

1
Pethidine 70mg from 10g/200mL
▾
  1. SR = 70 mg. SS = 10 g in 200 mL.
  2. Convert: 10 g × 1000 = 10,000 mg.
  3. Estimate: much less than 200 mL.
  4. Volume = (70 ÷ 10,000) × 200 = 1.4 mL
✅ Answer: 1.4 mL
2
Drip rate: 1.5L over 10h, 20 drops/mL
▾
  1. Volume = 1.5L → 1500mL. Drops/mL = 20. Time = 10h.
  2. Estimate ≈ 50 dpm.
  3. DPM = (1500 × 20) ÷ (10 × 60) = 50 dpm
✅ Answer: 50 dpm
3
750mg ciprofloxacin, 500mg tablets
▾
  1. SR = 750mg. SS = 500mg/tablet. Stock vol = 1.
  2. Estimate: between 1–2 tablets.
  3. Tablets = (750 ÷ 500) × 1 = 1.5
✅ Answer: 1.5 tablets
4
Volume: 100mL/h for 1.5h
▾
  1. Rate = 100mL/h, Time = 1.5h.
  2. Volume = 100 × 1.5 = 150mL
✅ Answer: 150 mL
5
Time: 2L dextrose saline at 160mL/h
▾
  1. Volume = 2L → 2000mL. Rate = 160mL/h.
  2. Time = 2000 ÷ 160 = 12.5h
✅ Answer: 12.5 hours (12h 30min)

Interactive Calculators

💉 Dose / Volume Calculator
—mL required
💧 IV Drip Rate Calculator
—drops per minute (dpm)
⏱️ Infusion Time / Volume / Rate
—result
Key conversionsg → mg (×1000) · L → mL (×1000) · Always estimate before calculating · Tablets: volume of stock = 1.
⚠️ Think Sepsis — Act FastEscalate immediately if suspected and initiate the bundle within the first hour.

Adult Symptoms — SEPSIS Mnemonic

  • SSlurred speech or confusion
  • EExtreme shivering or muscle pain
  • PPassing no urine in a day
  • SSevere breathlessness
  • I"It feels like you're going to die" — impending doom
  • SSkin mottled, discoloured or pale

Child Symptoms

  • 🔴Has a fit or convulsion
  • 🔵Looks mottled, bluish or pale
  • 🔴Non-blanching rash
  • 🟡Very lethargic / difficult to wake
  • ❄️Feels abnormally cold to touch

Additional Indicators

Fever >38°C or <36°C HR >90 bpm RR >20/min WBC >12 or <4 ×10⁹/L Altered mental status Hypotension (MAP <65) Lactate >2 mmol/L

1-Hour Sepsis Bundle

1
Measure Lactate — remeasure if >2 mmol/L
2
Blood Cultures — before antibiotics, ≥2 sets
3
Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics — IV, immediately
4
IV Fluid Resuscitation — 30mL/kg if hypotensive / lactate ≥4
5
Vasopressors — noradrenaline if MAP <65 despite fluids
6
Monitor & Reassess — obs, urine output, repeat lactate

qSOFA Quick Screen (≥2 = high risk)

Altered mentation
GCS < 15
Respiratory rate
≥ 22/min
Systolic BP
≤ 100 mmHg
⚡ Epi First — Epi Fast!Adrenaline is first-line. Use immediately. Adult: 0.5mg IM anterolateral thigh — no IV bolus.

Common Triggers

🥜 Food
PeanutsTree nutsShellfish/FishEggs/Milk
💊 Medication
PenicillinNSAIDs/Aspirin
🐝 Venom
Bees/WaspsFire ants
🧤 Latex
GlovesBalloons

Symptoms by Body System

🫁 Respiratory
Tightness, hoarseness, stridor, wheeze, SOB
💓 Cardiovascular
Weak/rapid pulse, hypotension, syncope, arrest
🩺 Skin
Itching, hives, flushing, angioedema
🫃 GI
Vomiting, diarrhoea, cramping

Emergency Management Steps

1
Remove trigger / call for help

Stop infusion, call MET/emergency team.

2
Adrenaline IM

Adult: 0.5mg IM anterolateral thigh. Repeat 5–15min PRN. No IV bolus.

3
Position patient

Lie flat, legs elevated if hypotensive. Sit up if respiratory distress.

4
High-flow O₂ + airway

100% O₂ via non-rebreather. Prepare for intubation if needed.

5
IV access + fluids

Large bore IV. Crystalloid 20mL/kg bolus for hypotension.

6
Monitor & reassess

Continuous obs, SpO₂, ECG. Watch for biphasic reaction (up to 72h).

⚠️ Refractory anaphylaxisNo improvement after 2 IM doses → escalate to ICU. Consider IV titrated adrenaline, second vasopressor, glucagon (if on beta-blockers).
